trajectory.description: Observation: Fresh start, no files yet. Analysis: Need to build a regression model for ClimSim atmospheric physics emulation. The task is to predict 128 output targets from 124 input features. This is a large dataset (~10M training samples). I'll start with a simple but effective MLP architecture, train for a few epochs to get a baseline, then iterate. Key decisions: 1) Use a deep MLP with residual connections, 2) Use DataLoader with multiple workers for efficiency, 3) Start with 5 epochs to measure timing, 4) Submit eval immediately to get baseline score.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline MLP with 512 hidden dim, 6 blocks achieved val_r2=0.9178 after 5 epochs (~85s/epoch). Each epoch takes ~85s, so 5 epochs = ~7.5 min. Analysis: The model is still improving (loss declining). I should submit eval to get baseline score, then scale up. With ~3.86h remaining and ~85s/epoch, I can do roughly 160 more epochs total. Key improvements to try: 1) Larger model (hidden_dim=1024, more blocks), 2) Train longer. Let me first submit eval to get baseline, then train a larger model for more epochs.

trajectory.description: Observation: mean_r2 = -767M due to r2_ptend_q = -1.77B. The specific humidity tendency outputs have near-zero variance at upper atmosphere levels, causing catastrophic R² when model predicts non-zero values. Analysis: Need to identify low-variance outputs and either zero them out or down-weight them in loss. The ClimSim paper uses per-output R² averaging, so near-zero variance outputs with any prediction error dominate negatively. Fix: compute per-output variance from training data, zero out predictions for outputs with near-zero variance (std < threshold), and use variance-weighted MSE loss. Decision: Implement this fix, increase model size to hidden_dim=1024, train for 20 epochs.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter_3 trained with variance-weighted loss, val_r2=0.5729 (internal weighted metric). The predict.py now zeros out near-zero variance outputs. Need to submit eval to verify the fix works. Analysis: The zero_mask approach should fix the catastrophic R² issue. But I also notice the variance-weighted loss is causing instability and lower performance than simple MSE (iter_1 got 0.9178 internal R²). I should switch back to simple MSE loss but keep the zero_mask for predictions. Also need to fix the val_r2 computation to use true unweighted mean R². Decision: Submit eval with current checkpoint first, then retrain with simple MSE + zero_mask.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter_7 training went catastrophically wrong - val_r2 went to -1M during training because resuming with new cosine LR schedule destabilized the model. The zero_mask isn't applied during val_r2 computation, so near-zero variance outputs cause catastrophic R². Best checkpoint is still 0.5800 from iter_5. Analysis: Two fixes needed: 1) Apply zero_mask during val_r2 computation so it's stable, 2) Resume from best checkpoint with very low LR (1e-4) and proper warmup. Also the val_loss is increasing (overfitting), so I should use a smaller LR. Decision: Fix training script to apply zero_mask in val_r2 computation, resume from best checkpoint with lr=5e-4, train 20 more epochs.

trajectory.description: Observation: Model overfits - train_loss decreases but val_loss increases after epoch 7. Best val_r2=0.5800 from iter_5. The bottleneck is r2_ptend_q=0.3192. Analysis: Need stronger regularization. The model overfits quickly. I'll train from scratch with dropout=0.1, higher weight_decay=1e-4, and use OneCycleLR. Also try a larger hidden_dim=1536 with 10 blocks. With 1.71h remaining (~53 epochs at 115s/epoch), I'll train 12 epochs to get a better model. Key insight: the vertical structure matters - I should try a model that processes vertical levels more carefully. Decision: Train fresh model with better regularization for 12 epochs.
